transcription_cleaned:
Doctor: Good morning. How can I help you today?

Patient: Good morning, doctor. I was referred here from another hospital. I'm 27 weeks pregnant and I had a very scary episode a few weeks ago.

Doctor: I'm glad you came in. Can you walk me through what happened?

Patient: At 22 weeks, I started having vaginal bleeding. I went to the emergency room and they admitted me for bedrest. About two days later, I got terrible pelvic pain and contractions, and I passed a large mass vaginally.

Doctor: That sounds very distressing. Were there any fetal parts in the mass?

Patient: No, the doctors said there were no fetal contents. The baby was fine — they checked the heart tones and everything was okay.

Doctor: Do you know what the pathology showed on that mass?

Patient: Yes, they told me it was a very aggressive-looking tumor under the microscope, with spindle-shaped cells and giant cells. The pathologist said it could be an endometrial stromal sarcoma or an undifferentiated uterine sarcoma, but they weren't sure.

Doctor: I see. And what happened after that — did they do any procedure at the outside hospital?

Patient: Yes, they took me to the operating room and found a hole in the back wall of my vagina that was bleeding. They stitched it closed. My cervix was closed and the baby was still okay.

Doctor: Good. Can you tell me about your obstetric history?

Patient: This is my fourth pregnancy. I've had two therapeutic abortions and one miscarriage.

Doctor: Any prior gynecologic problems — abnormal pap smears, fibroids, infertility?

Patient: No, nothing like that. My periods were always regular, no pain with sex, no unusual bleeding before this pregnancy.

Doctor: Any family history of cancer?

Patient: Yes, actually. Two of my family members had cervical cancer and two had endometrial cancer.

Doctor: That's important to know. We'll order an MRI today to get a better picture of what's happening. Has the fetus had any issues noted on ultrasound?

Patient: Yes, one of the baby's kidneys looks abnormal — they said it's dysplastic.

Doctor: Okay. And how are you feeling now at 27 weeks?

Patient: I feel okay. No more bleeding, no pelvic pain. I'm just worried about what this mass was.

Doctor: Understood. We're going to coordinate with our gynecologic oncology and maternal-fetal medicine teams to make the best plan for you and your baby.

Patient: Thank you, doctor. I just want to make sure we're both safe.
clinical_summary: A 31-year-old G4P0 woman, currently 27 weeks pregnant, presented with vaginal bleeding and expulsion of a large pelvic mass at 22 weeks gestation. The mass was initially suspected to be a sarcoma, and a posterior vaginal wall defect was repaired. Her obstetric history includes two therapeutic abortions and one miscarriage, and there is a family history of cervical and endometrial cancer. Current medications include betamethasone for fetal lung maturity. An MRI showed no metastatic disease, and a multidisciplinary team plans a cesarean hysterectomy at 36 weeks. The fetus has a unilateral multi-cystic dysplastic kidney.

differential_diagnosis.rationale: The expulsion of a large pelvic mass during pregnancy, initially suspected to be a sarcoma, aligns with the eventual diagnosis of high-grade vaginal sarcoma, which is consistent with the patient's history and the ER/PR positive status.

Vaginal bleeding during pregnancy, especially with a significant event like the expulsion of a mass, raises the possibility of placental abruption, although the presence of a mass makes this less likely.

Given the family history of cervical cancer and the presentation of vaginal bleeding with a pelvic mass, cervical cancer could be considered, although the diagnosis of vaginal sarcoma is more consistent with the clinical findings.

final_report.subjective: The patient, a 31-year-old gravida 4 para 0 woman, is currently 27 weeks pregnant and presents with a history of a significant obstetric event at 22 weeks gestation. She reports experiencing vaginal bleeding and the expulsion of a large pelvic mass, which was initially suspected to be a sarcoma. The patient describes the mass as being devoid of fetal parts, and subsequent evaluations confirmed the fetus was unharmed. She underwent surgical intervention to repair a posterior vaginal wall defect. Her obstetric history includes two therapeutic abortions and one miscarriage. She has no prior gynecologic issues such as abnormal pap smears or fibroids. The patient expresses concern about the nature of the mass and its implications for her pregnancy. She also notes a family history of cervical and endometrial cancer, which adds to her anxiety about her condition.
final_report.objective: On examination, the patient appears well and reports no current symptoms of bleeding or pelvic pain. Her obstetric history is significant for a fetal anomaly, specifically a unilateral multi-cystic dysplastic kidney identified on prenatal ultrasound. A recent MRI showed no evidence of metastatic disease, and a multidisciplinary team has been involved in her care. The pelvic examination reveals no residual vaginal mass, and a pap smear conducted at 27 weeks was normal with negative HPV results. The patient is currently receiving betamethasone to promote fetal lung maturity in preparation for a planned cesarean hysterectomy at 36 weeks gestation.
final_report.assessment: The primary concern is the management of a suspected high-grade vaginal sarcoma, initially identified as a large pelvic mass expelled at 22 weeks gestation. The differential diagnosis includes vaginal sarcoma, placental abruption, and cervical cancer, though the clinical findings and pathology reports support the diagnosis of a sarcoma. The patient's family history of gynecologic cancers and the ER/PR positive status of the tumor are significant considerations in her ongoing management. The fetus's condition, specifically the dysplastic kidney, is also being monitored closely as part of the comprehensive care plan.
final_report.plan: The plan involves continued coordination with gynecologic oncology and maternal-fetal medicine specialists. The patient will undergo a cesarean hysterectomy at 36 weeks to manage the sarcoma and ensure the safety of both mother and child. Betamethasone has been administered to aid in fetal lung development. Post-surgical pathology will be crucial in determining the presence of any malignant cells and guiding further treatment. The patient will also receive adjuvant high-dose-rate vaginal brachytherapy postoperatively to address any residual disease. Regular follow-ups and imaging will be essential to monitor for disease recurrence and manage the fetal renal anomaly.
